metadata.seed: I was already having a really bad day, from a run in I had with a very rude lady. Then I received a text from someone who I thought was my friend. She felt like I haven' t been there for her because I didn't pick up her call. I honestly didn't see her call and definitely would've got back to her, if I saw her call. She said mean and nasty things about me, which I don' t care about. But she did the wrong mistake of bringing my child into the equation, when he had nothing to do with it. I wanted to really beat the crap out of her because not only did she do that, she blocked my number and ran. Like I was beyond pissed.

patient.query: I was already having a really bad day, from a run in I had with a very rude lady. Then I received a text from someone who I thought was my friend. She felt like I haven' t been there for her because I didn't pick up her call. I honestly didn't see her call and definitely would've got back to her, if I saw her call. She said mean and nasty things about me, which I don' t care about. But she did the wrong mistake of bringing my child into the equation, when he had nothing to do with it. I wanted to really beat the crap out of her because not only did she do that, she blocked my number and ran. Like I was beyond pissed.

triggers: run in with a rude lady
text from someone I thought was my friend
friend felt I haven't been there for her
mean and nasty things said about me
my child being brought into the equation
automatic_thoughts: I would have got back to her if I saw her call
I wanted to beat her up
behaviors: feeling like I need to react

neutral: Worried about being a bad mother (finding)

llm_response.response:
I hear how upset and angry you’re feeling after that encounter.  
What evidence do you have that she was deliberately trying to hurt you, and how might you know if she was simply upset or misinformed?  
Have you considered how a different reaction—perhaps a calm message or a brief pause—might change the outcome for both you and your child?

patient.query:
Yeah, I mean, I guess I just felt really blindsided by what she said. It seemed so personal and harsh at the time, like she was attacking me almost. But I suppose I don't really know what was going on for her. Maybe she was just having a bad day or didn't have all the facts. 

Thinking back, a calmer response might have helped... but in the moment, everything just felt so overwhelming. It's hard to pause when emotions are running high, you know?

automatic_thoughts: I don't know what was going on for her

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Tends to exaggerate emotional expression inappropriately (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how intense that moment felt for you. What clues or details led you to think she was attacking you? How might you view what she said if you considered other possible reasons for her tone or words?

patient.query:
I guess it was the way she raised her voice and the words she chose. It felt like she was pointing fingers, like I was the problem. But I do wonder if maybe she was just really frustrated about something else. 

It’s just hard for me not to take it personally; I tend to feel like I’m always the one who messes things up. But maybe it was more about her than me? I don’t know, it just feels confusing sometimes.

automatic_thoughts: maybe it was more about her than me

entailment: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
neutral: Tends to be intolerant of frustration (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of confusion and self-blame. What evidence do you have that the situation was about you versus her frustration? How might you look at the situation from a different angle?

patient.query:
Yeah, I mean, I guess I don’t really have solid evidence that it was all about me. It’s just the instinct I have—you know, to think I did something wrong. I always feel like I have to keep everything in check, and when things go sideways, it feels like it’s my fault.

Trying to see it from her perspective is tough, though. I can get that she might be dealing with her own stuff, but it’s hard to not feel hurt in the moment

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight when things don’t go as planned. What evidence do you have that the situation was entirely your fault? Could there be other factors or perspectives that might also explain what happened?

patient.query:
I guess I don’t have clear evidence, just that gut feeling that I messed up. It’s like this inner voice that always assumes I’m to blame when things go wrong. 

But thinking about it, I know she has her own issues, and maybe I was just a trigger for her. Still, it's hard to shake off that feeling of guilt. It makes me question myself and my actions all the time, and that just adds to the pressure.

automatic_thoughts: I question myself and my actions

llm_response.response: It sounds like that sense of guilt is really heavy for you. What specific thoughts or moments make you feel you might have messed up? Are there other ways you could view what happened that might ease that pressure?

patient.query:
I don’t have solid proof that it was my fault—it's just this nagging feeling that I always mess things up. There's that inner voice telling me that I should’ve done better in the moment, that I should've seen it coming. 

Thinking about her struggles helps a little, but the hurt lingers. It makes me question my worth and why I can’t seem to get things right with my friends. It's exhausting, you know?

automatic_thoughts: I can't seem to get things right with my friends

llm_response.response: It sounds like that inner voice is weighing heavily on you—what specific moments make you feel like you’re “messing things up”? Have you noticed any times when you handled a situation well, even if it didn’t go exactly as you’d hoped? How might you view the situation if you were talking to a close friend who was in your shoes?

patient.query:
Sometimes it’s just the little things, like forgetting to check in with someone or saying something that doesn’t come out right. I know I can get lost in my thoughts and not realize how my words might land on others. 

I wish I could say there are times I felt proud of how I handled things, but I mostly remember the times I stumbled. If a friend were in my shoes, I’d probably tell them it’s okay to mess up and that they're doing their best. But when

triggers: forgetting to check in with someone
saying something that doesn’t come out right
automatic_thoughts: I can get lost in my thoughts
my words might land poorly on others
I mostly remember the times I stumbled

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of shame from those moments that didn’t go as planned. What are some times—maybe even small ones—when you handled a situation well or felt you did your best? How might those moments change the way you view yourself right now?

patient.query:
Yeah, I guess I do carry a lot of that shame. It feels like the weight of all the times I could’ve done better. But… thinking back, there are a few times I managed to handle things okay. Like that time I reached out to a friend who was going through a rough patch, and I was actually able to listen and support her without trying to fix everything. 

In those moments, I felt connected and like I was really there for someone, and it reminded me that

automatic_thoughts: I managed to handle things okay
I was able to listen and support her

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re weighing the weight of past regrets against moments where you felt truly helpful. What do you think made those supportive moments feel different or more successful for you? How might you use those insights to shift the way you view the times you feel you “could’ve done better”?

patient.query:
Yeah, I guess I carry a lot of that shame. It feels heavy, like the weight of all the times I could’ve done better. But sometimes, I think back to a few moments when I handled things okay. 

Like that time I reached out to a friend who needed support, and I just listened to her without trying to fix everything. In those moments, I felt connected, like I was really there for her, and it reminded me that maybe I can do this friendship thing right

automatic_thoughts: maybe I can do this friendship thing right

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

patient.query:
I guess I carry a lot of shame. It feels heavy, like the weight of all the times I could’ve done better. But sometimes, I think back to moments when I handled things okay.

Like when I reached out to a friend who needed support, and I just listened to her. In those moments, I felt connected, like I was really there for her. It reminded me that maybe I can do this friendship thing right sometimes, even if it doesn’t feel like it most of the

automatic_thoughts: I can do this friendship thing right sometimes

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear you carrying a heavy sense of shame tied to past mistakes, yet you also notice moments of connection when you supported a friend.
